turns.content: I'm building a tool to automate file renaming and I keep wondering what's the difference between using vec![name; 100] versus calling extend with a range iterator?

The key distinction here is that vec![expr; n] creates an immediate vector of size n where every element is exactly expr, whereas extend pulls from another collection. If you need to collect filenames from an IntoIterator like std::fs::FileDir, use Windows::collect pattern or simply extend your Vec with the iterator rather than preallocating fixed values. For instance, let mut files = Vec::new(); files.extend(file_dir); is more idiomatic than assuming how many files exist upfront.
That makes sense for collecting, but what if I'm filtering a massive list of config items and only want the valid ones? Should I use retain or drain instead?

You should definitely use retain when you need to keep some elements and discard others in place. Think of it like this: imagine your Vec is a hallway where everyone holds a badge, and retain acts as the bouncer who lets only people with valid badges pass through while removing the rest from the corridor without clearing the entire space first. In contrast, drain empties everything into another container before you can add new items back in, which is useful when you're moving data between collections rather than filtering within one.
I have a list of 10,000 items and I want to pre-allocate the vector to avoid reallocations. Should I use vec![item; n] or push_back every time?

Use with_capacity for the size but remember that vec![expr; n] is more idiomatic when you already have a uniform value like an integer or string literal. If you are constructing complex objects, avoid creating temporary allocations inside the vector initializer and instead use extend or push to add them one by one after setting capacity.
Okay so I'll pre-allocate with_capacity but then I need to filter out invalid entries from that vector. Should I just iterate and keep valid ones? Or is there a better way using retain?

Use the retain method which moves elements in place based on a closure predicate, this avoids creating an intermediate collection entirely. However if you are filtering based on ownership or need to move items out of the vector into another container, drain can be useful here along with split_at if you want to divide the vector at a specific index point.
Wait so I should just use retain? But what about duplicate entries in my data that I need to remove? Should I combine dedup with retain or run them separately?

Run them separately because dedup creates a new vector which loses the original order and ownership semantics, while retain keeps order. If you need both filtering and removing duplicates while preserving order, consider using windows::collect pattern over an iterator that checks for consecutive duplicate elements before collecting them into a final vec.

I'm building a list of user IDs from the database and currently doing vec![id; count] to preallocate memory, but I'm worried this might be wasteful if some IDs are invalid. Is there a better way to handle filtering without losing performance?

Using retain is definitely the right approach here since it lets you filter in place while keeping the original capacity. You could do vec![id; count].retain(|x| x.is_valid()) to remove invalid entries, but that still allocates the full size upfront which might be wasteful if most IDs fail validation.

That sounds efficient until I realize I'm still allocating for all those invalid IDs. Can't I just iterate through the raw data and only add valid ones to a new Vec? Would that avoid the wasted memory?

You're absolutely right, but there's a catch with iterators in Rust. If you want to collect into a Vec while preserving order, you can use extend on an empty vector or even better, start with vec![id; count] and then call retain only if you know most IDs will be valid. Otherwise, consider using Windows::collect pattern where you iterate over the input and push each valid item directly without preallocating for invalid ones.
I know how to use a vector for basic storage, but I'm confused about when to use with_capacity versus push. It feels like they do the same thing to me.

The confusion is common because both functions grow the vector, but their underlying mechanics differ significantly. With capacity pre-allocates memory using Vec::with_capacity(n), which reserves space upfront and avoids reallocations entirely if you fill it later. Push adds elements one by one, forcing the allocator to resize the buffer when full, which triggers a reallocation and copy of existing data for every Nth insertion. The choice depends on whether your workload is known ahead of time or dynamic.
Okay so I should always reserve space if I know how many items I need? But what if I'm wrong about the count later? Does with_capacity prevent reallocation completely even after I stop pushing?

If you call with_capacity(n) and then push beyond n elements, the vector will still attempt to resize its internal buffer. The capacity argument only guarantees that space for exactly n items is available initially; it does not lock in memory forever. If you need guaranteed no reallocations regardless of input size, you must choose between either using a fixed-size array or ensuring your actual count never exceeds the reserved capacity.

I'm porting my old Python list code into Rust, and I thought I'd just use the vec! macro like it's a magic shortcut to create an array of repeated values. But when I tried using vec![item; count] with actual data instead of a constant expression, it crashed immediately.

That's a common misunderstanding for beginners because the vec! macro is only valid in compile-time contexts where every element must be known at startup. When you try to use it with runtime data or complex expressions, the compiler rejects it because it can't verify the repetition pattern. You need to start thinking about how Vec actually grows and shrinks rather than treating it like a fixed-size array.

I'm so frustrated right now because I spent hours trying to fix this and my program just won't compile. Can you explain what the actual error is telling me? It says something about invalid repetition but I don't understand why.

The error tells you that vec![expr; n] requires expr to be a constant, so if your expression depends on runtime values like loop counters or function results, the compiler can't guarantee it's safe. Instead of using vec!, you should use push() to add items one by one, or pre-allocate with capacity() and then extend(). If you need to filter data while building a new collection, retain() is actually much more idiomatic than trying to recreate lists manually.

I've read about vec![expr; n] and with_capacity a dozen times, but my code still feels wrong when I'm under pressure. It seems like the compiler is just ignoring what I write because I don't understand the underlying mechanics of Vec.

The real reason you're struggling isn't about syntax; it's that with_capacity actually pre-allocates memory for the elements, whereas vec![expr; n] only creates a vector filled with copies. Imagine building a warehouse: with_capacity is like buying an empty container and hiring workers to fill it yourself at your own pace, while vec![expr; n] is ordering a fully stocked box where everything inside is already identical. When you need to filter data, retain is the idiomatic way because it keeps only elements that satisfy a predicate rather than manually looping through items.
Oh I see now, but I keep thinking I should use extend instead of retain. Can't I just append everything to a new vector? It feels safer because it won't mess up my existing data structure.

Extend is actually perfect for appending items from another collection or iterating over something else, but if you're filtering based on conditions like removing duplicates, extend doesn't do that. Think of it this way: extend is like adding new boxes to your shelf while retain is a quality control process where you inspect every box and throw away the bad ones before keeping only the good ones in the final list.
